_comment: PHASE C-1 - MALVERN HILL, July 1, 1862 - the remaining Eastern marquee battle for the __FIELD real-time engine (single-phase, the Fredericksburg/Shiloh pattern). The Seven Days ended on a modest open plateau north of the James River, where Fitz John Porter and artillery chief Henry J. Hunt turned Malvern Hill into a killing ground. Lee attempted to silence the Union guns with badly coordinated Confederate grand batteries, then sent piecemeal infantry attacks across open slopes. The battle is modeled as a SINGLE-PHASE Confederate assault against a Union gun line: CS attacks from the high-z woods and roads; US defends the Crew house ridge / Malvern plateau at low-mid z. ARTILLERY uses the D75 UNIVERSAL GUN MODEL (guns * the universal weight, crew-scaled) with no battle-specific damage constants. ANTI-LOST-CAUSE: the lesson is not Confederate valor or Union timidity in isolation; it is command failure, staff confusion, ineffective Confederate artillery concentration, Hunt's professional gun handling, Porter's field command, and McClellan's retreat from a tactical victory to Harrison's Landing. Sources: NPS Richmond National Battlefield Park; NPS History Civil War Series 'The Battles for Richmond, 1862'; American Battlefield Trust Malvern Hill article, battle summary, and map; Encyclopedia Virginia; OR-derived OOB summaries. Strengths and exact arrival minutes are Inferred at brigade/division abstraction scale.

weather.note: Union guns crowned the hill; the day's rain held off until July 2 — the haze here was powder-smoke, not sky.
sources: American Battlefield Trust, 'Malvern Hill' battle page (battlefields.org)
National Park Service, Richmond National Battlefield Park, 'The Battle of Malvern Hill' (nps.gov/rich)

malvernHill.name: Malvern Hill - the Seven Days' Artillery Duel
malvernHill.place: Malvern Hill, Henrico County, Virginia - near the James River
malvernHill.blurb: The Seven Days end in a roar on an open plateau north of the James. Porter holds the crest while Henry Hunt masses the Army of the Potomac's guns along Crew house ridge; Lee tries to break the position with grand batteries and then with infantry attacks fed in by fragment and rumor. Cross the open fields under the Union gun line as the Confederacy, or hold the hill and prove that artillery, terrain, and command discipline can stop Lee's first great offensive.
malvernHill.provenance: Verified (high) for date, place, commanders, the open slope, the Union tactical victory, Hunt's massed artillery, Lee's failed two-grand-battery plan, Magruder's late/piecemeal attacks, D.H. Hill's center assault, the James River gunboat context, and the casualty asymmetry. Inferred (medium) for per-unit strengths, exact field minutes, and the brigade-scale compression. Sources: NPS Richmond National Battlefield Park; NPS History Civil War Series 'The Battles for Richmond, 1862'; American Battlefield Trust Malvern Hill article/summary/map; Encyclopedia Virginia; OR-derived OOB summaries and modern battlefield scholarship including Gallagher/Burton/O'Reilly.
malvernHill._e52Note: E52 (D254): accurate-inputs defense correction. The shipped US 8/8 was clock-manufactured (D252: at the buzzer the CS stood mid-hold on the Crew-house objective on EVERY seed with the US at zero in the radius). The crest infantry was ~2.6x under-modeled: Sears (To the Gates of Richmond, pp. 311-312) puts ~17,800 men of Morell's + Couch's divisions on the northern face (model had 6,800); corrected US infantry totals 26,800 = American Battlefield Trust's ~27,000 engaged. The CS assault is raised honestly the same pass: NPS + ABT independently give ~30,000 CS engaged ('twenty brigades under seven division commanders'); the per-division split beyond D.H. Hill's sourced 8,200 is an Inferred allocation to that Verified aggregate (Jackson-wing 5,900 / Magruder 6,300 / Huger 4,500), with Whiting deliberately NOT raised (documented support posture, not an assault wave). Added: Tyler's heavy guns near the Malvern house (16 - the Sears p. 312 composition; the competing 'ten' total unconfirmable - Disputed) and Meagher's Irish Brigade (~1,100, reached Couch ~7pm). Sickles's brigade is documented present but NOT added - no engaged strength exists (D92: never invent). Gunboats stay label-and-teaching-only (sourced verdict: imprecise, marginal, friendly fire, ceased). Full trail: docs/design/e52-malvern-research.md. Direction emerges from these inputs under the E48 rule - never tuned (D74).
malvernHill._homeEdgeNote: E47 (D240): role-aware home edges. This battle seats the US defender at LOW z (the Crew house ridge, US reinforcements entering z 145-300) and the CS attacker at HIGH z (the woods, CS reinforcements z 735-860), the inverse of the engine's default side-keyed edges — so before this field, a routing unit fled THROUGH the enemy line toward the wrong edge, inflating defender casualties. Rout, fallback, officer-ride, and supply-train direction all follow this declaration.
malvernHill._assaultDoctrineNote: The AI Confederate attacker models Malvern Hill's disjointed frontal assaults: it presses in line across the open ground and trades under the guns, but it does not get the modern B-1 column rush/massed bayonet solution that would erase Lee's command failure. A human Confederate player can still attempt a better-coordinated attack.

menu.title: MALVERN HILL (1862)
menu.deck: The Seven Days' culminating artillery duel - command either side: hold Hunt and Porter's gun line on the open plateau, or try to coordinate Lee's shattered assaults across the killing fields.
menu.aria: Malvern Hill, July 1862 - the Seven Days' culminating artillery duel. Command either army: hold the Union gun line on Malvern Hill or attack it with Lee's army.

sides.heading: Malvern Hill - Take Which Command?
sides.intro: July 1, 1862 - the final battle of the Seven Days. Lead the army of your choosing.

US.title: Command the Union (Porter's line)
US.deck: Hold the Crew house ridge with Porter's infantry and Hunt's massed guns. Keep the batteries firing, feed fresh divisions into the line, and stop Lee's assaults short of the crest.

CS.title: Command the Confederacy (Lee's army)
CS.deck: Bring order to a confused attack. Silence the Federal guns, push Armistead, Magruder, and D.H. Hill forward together, and try to take the hill before darkness ends the Seven Days.

sides.foot: The other army is commanded by the AI. The historical Confederate attack was piecemeal and badly coordinated; press V in battle to toggle fog.

brief.attack: Your guns have not silenced Hunt's ridge, and the open ground ahead is swept by Federal artillery. Push the infantry through the ravines and across the plateau before darkness ends Lee's offensive. This is your war: make a coordinated assault out of the chaos that historically became murder.
brief.defend: Hold the open crest with infantry behind the batteries. Hunt's guns dominate the slopes, Porter is the field commander, and the James River is at your back. This is your war: turn Malvern Hill's fields of fire into a real tactical victory and deny Lee the climactic blow he wanted.

objective.name: Crew House Ridge and the Malvern Plateau
objective.note: The open crest around the Crew house and Willis Church Road. The defender doctrine's forward face sits on the gun line near z 396, while the Confederate attacker must cross the open slope from the woods and ravines. Historically Hunt's guns and Porter's infantry held this plateau; Lee's attacks stopped short.

leaders._note: fate = a per-leader durability multiplier weighting the casualty model to history. Porter, Hunt, Lee, Jackson, Magruder, D.H. Hill, and Armistead survived Malvern Hill; the battle's command lesson is disordered control, not a scripted famous-death event.

US.name: Brig. Gen. Fitz John Porter
US.note: Porter's V Corps anchored the western ridge and he functioned as the de facto field commander while McClellan was away from the immediate front. His placement of infantry and reserves made the ridge a coherent position. (Verified - rank corrected: he was a BRIGADIER general at Malvern Hill; his major-generalcy is dated July 4, 1862, three days AFTER the battle, the same July-4 date the file correctly withholds from Couch, Morell, and Kearny - do not re-promote by analogy to his later rank.)
US.teach: Gen. Porter steadies the ridge - the field commander who makes the position fight as a system.

US.name: Col. Henry J. Hunt
US.note: Commander of the Artillery Reserve, Army of the Potomac. At Malvern Hill he concentrated the guns, shifted batteries as they exhausted ammunition, and crushed Confederate counter-battery efforts. (Verified - rank AND title corrected, E52: he was a COLONEL commanding the Artillery Reserve at the battle; both his brigadier-generalcy and the formal Chief-of-Artillery post date Sept 15, 1862 - the file already calls him 'Col.' in us_parrott_line.)
US.teach: Col. Hunt massed the guns - professional artillery command turns open ground into a defensive machine.

US.name: Brig. Gen. Darius N. Couch
US.note: Couch's division held the center/right of the Union line and reinforced the plateau as the attacks came in. (Verified)
US.teach: Gen. Couch feeds the line - the center holds because reserves arrive before the crest breaks.

US.name: Brig. Gen. George W. Morell
US.note: Morell's division of Porter's V Corps held the left/western ridge near the Crew house. (Verified)
US.teach: Gen. Morell's division hugs the gun line - infantry and artillery hold as one arm.

CS.name: Gen. Robert E. Lee
CS.note: Lee wanted one final blow before McClellan reached the James, but staff work and command coordination failed him. Malvern Hill is a major early example of Lee's offensive aggression turning costly. (Verified)
CS.teach: Gen. Lee's final blow misfires - boldness without staff control becomes a ruinous frontal attack.

CS.name: Maj. Gen. John B. Magruder
CS.note: Magruder's guides misled him; his division arrived late, and he launched attacks under confusing and erroneous orders. (Verified)
CS.teach: Gen. Magruder reaches the field late - the attack starts by rumor rather than design.

CS.name: Maj. Gen. D. H. Hill
CS.note: Hill warned that Malvern Hill should be left alone if McClellan held it in strength. His division nevertheless advanced into the center late in the day and suffered heavily. (Verified)
CS.teach: Gen. D.H. Hill sees the trap - and still his division is fed into it.

CS.name: Brig. Gen. Lewis A. Armistead
CS.note: Armistead's brigade moved first against Union skirmishers and became the mistaken signal for a larger attack. He survived Malvern Hill; his more famous mortal wound came at Gettysburg in 1863. (Verified)
CS.teach: Gen. Armistead's advance is mistaken for success - a skirmisher fight becomes the signal for disaster.

CS.name: Maj. Gen. Thomas J. 'Stonewall' Jackson
CS.note: Jackson's wing was finally in place on Lee's left, but his Seven Days performance remained sluggish and uncharacteristic. His troops could not turn the ridge. (Verified - rank corrected: he was a MAJOR general at Malvern Hill; the Confederate lieutenant-general grade did not exist until October 1862 - the same Hood-at-Chickamauga anachronism, avoided here.)
CS.teach: Gen. Jackson's wing is present at last - but the offensive machine still cannot coordinate.

US.name: Union train - Malvern House and the James River road

CS.name: Confederate train - Willis Church Road and the Richmond approaches

hills.name: Malvern Hill plateau
hills.note: A modest elevation, roughly a hundred feet above the surrounding ground. Its strength was not height alone but open fields of fire. (Verified)

hills.name: Crew House ridge
hills.note: The western ridge near the Cornelius Crew house where Porter's line and Hunt's batteries dominated the open slope. (Verified)

hills.name: Willis Church Road shoulder
hills.note: The road shoulder that framed the Federal center and the Confederate approach lanes. (Verified)

woods.note: The woods and ravines from which Armistead, Magruder, and D.H. Hill emerged before crossing the open slope. (Verified)
Western approach woods near Carter's Mill Road - the Confederate right's confused route toward the plateau. (Verified)
Northern/eastern approach woods near Jackson's front. (Verified)
Malvern Cliffs and broken ground on the Union left - difficult terrain that limited Huger's flank attack. (Verified)

markers.name: Willis Church Road
markers.note: The north-south road through Lee's headquarters area and the Union center. (Verified)

markers.name: Carter's Mill Road
markers.note: One of the Confederate approach routes; confusion along these roads fed the piecemeal attacks. (Verified)

markers.name: River Road to Harrison's Landing
markers.note: The road toward the James River base and the retreat McClellan ordered after the tactical victory. (Verified)

markers.name: Crew house ridge - Hunt's gun line
markers.note: Hunt's batteries straddled the ridge and Willis Church Road, firing west, north, and east. (Verified)

markers.name: open killing fields
markers.note: The barren slopes in front of the plateau. Confederate infantry crossed this ground under artillery and rifle fire. (Verified)

markers.name: Poindexter / Crew fields
markers.note: The farm fields where the Confederate attacks bogged down short of the crest. (Verified)

markers.name: to the James River gunboats
markers.note: Federal gunboats on the James lobbed heavy shells into Confederate positions; impressive and frightening, but not the main cause of the repulse. (Verified)

markers.name: Western Run and ravines
markers.note: Ravines and broken ground in front of the plateau disrupted Confederate formations before they reached open ground. (Verified)

US.name: Morell's Division (Porter's V Corps)
US.note: V Corps division holding the western ridge near the Crew house. E52: half of the ~17,800 Morell+Couch crest aggregate (Sears, To the Gates of Richmond pp. 311-312); no source splits the two divisions, so the even split is the honest minimum. (Verified identity; Inferred strength - sourced aggregate, unsourced split)

US.name: Couch's Division (IV Corps)
US.note: Couch's division strengthened the Union center/right and worked closely with Porter's line. E52: half of the ~17,800 Morell+Couch crest aggregate (Sears pp. 311-312); even split, see us_morell. Corrected US infantry totals 26,800, consistent with ABT's ~27,000 engaged. (Verified identity; Inferred strength - sourced aggregate, unsourced split)

US.name: Sykes's Regulars
US.note: Regular infantry in Porter's corps, held as a disciplined support behind the gun line; west slope toward the River Road, NOT part of the ~17,800 crest aggregate. E52: no sourced engaged strength found (honest dead end) - 2,500 retained. (Verified identity; Inferred strength)

US.name: Ames's Battery A, 5th U.S. Artillery
US.note: Ames's regular battery was one of the hard-firing Federal batteries at Malvern Hill. Universal gun model: 12 guns, realistic full detachment abstraction. (Verified battery role; Inferred aggregate gun count)

US.name: Hunt's Artillery Reserve (center)
US.note: The central mass of Hunt's reserve guns along the plateau. Universal gun model: 16 guns, realistic crew/detachment count. (Verified massed artillery; Inferred battery aggregation)

US.name: Reserve Artillery - Parrott and Napoleon line
US.note: Right-center batteries facing Jackson and D.H. Hill. Universal gun model: 12 guns. (Verified artillery mass; Inferred grouping)

US.name: Tyler's Siege Guns (1st Connecticut Heavy Artillery)
US.note: E52: the heavy-gun reserve on high ground near the Malvern house. Composition sourced to Sears p. 312: five 4.5-in Rodmans, five 20-pdr Parrotts, six 32-pdr howitzers = 16; the competing 'ten siege guns' total could not be source-confirmed (Disputed count - the sourced composition adopted). That they fired into the assaults - the 32-pounders cutting lanes through the charging infantry - rests on secondary accounts (Warfare History Network, Iron Brigader); the primary pull could not confirm the Tyler/1st-CT identity, and how many tubes were in action at once is undocumented. (Sears-sourced composition; Inferred identity and fire; Disputed total count)

CS.name: Armistead's Brigade
CS.note: Armistead's brigade moved first against Union skirmishers and became the mistaken signal for the wider attack. E52 verify-leg catch: sources describe 'three regiments (about half the brigade)' as the trigger advance - a deployed fraction, not a counted brigade total - so the 2,500 stays but its stamp is honest. (Verified identity and trigger role; Inferred strength)

CS.name: D.H. Hill's Division
CS.note: Hill's five-brigade division (Rodes/Gordon, G.B. Anderson, Garland, Colquitt, Ripley) advanced in the Confederate center near Crew's field and suffered heavily. E52: 8,200 is the division's Malvern-Hill-specific engaged figure (Burton, Extraordinary Circumstances, pp. 337-338); the ~12,300 sum of its brigades' rosters is June-26 paper strength (pre-Gaines's-Mill losses) and was rejected. (Verified identity; Inferred strength - Burton's engaged figure over the paper sum)

CS.name: Jackson's Wing
CS.note: Jackson's own and Ewell's brigades launched real late assaults on Lee's left - none came close to succeeding. E52: only Winder (~1,000) has a sourced count; 5,900 is an Inferred allocation toward the Verified ~30,000 CS engaged (NPS + ABT). (Verified identity; Inferred strength - allocation to a Verified aggregate. Rank: MAJ. GEN. at the battle - no CSA lieutenant-general grade until Oct 1862.)

CS.name: Confederate Left Grand Battery
CS.note: Part of Lee's intended crossfire. Historically the Confederate guns were poorly coordinated and quickly beaten by Hunt's counter-battery fire. Universal gun model: 8 guns. (Verified plan; Inferred aggregation)

CS.name: Confederate Right Grand Battery
CS.note: The other half of Lee's planned converging artillery fire. Poor staff work and Union counter-battery fire prevented a real crossfire. Universal gun model: 8 guns. (Verified plan; Inferred aggregation)

reinforcements.name: Kearny's Division (III Corps reserve)
reinforcements.entry: from the reserve behind the hill
reinforcements.note: Kearny's division was part of the Union depth behind the line. It represents the fresh infantry Porter could feed forward. (Verified context; Inferred timing)

reinforcements.name: Hooker's Division (III Corps reserve)
reinforcements.entry: from the reserve on the James River road
reinforcements.note: Hooker's division formed part of the mass of reserve infantry behind Malvern Hill. (Verified context; Inferred timing. Rank: BRIG. GEN. at the battle - his major-generalcy of volunteers was ISSUED July 26, 1862 and backdated to May 5, 1862, so like Porter, Couch, and Kearny he was still a brigadier on the field July 1; the canonical Seven Days OOB lists him BG. The earlier D92 'promotion' to Maj. Gen. by date-of-rank was reverted as inconsistent with the file's own rule against promoting by analogy to later rank.)

reinforcements.name: Hunt's Relief Batteries
reinforcements.entry: fresh batteries moved onto the crest as ammunition wagons worked behind the line
reinforcements.note: Hunt shifted fresh batteries to keep the line firing. Universal gun model: 10 guns. (Verified practice; Inferred grouping)

reinforcements.name: Meagher's Irish Brigade (II Corps)
reinforcements.entry: sent by Sumner as the evening assaults crested, reaching Couch's line near dark
reinforcements.note: E52: Sumner sent the Irish Brigade to shore up Couch late in the day (reached the line ~7 p.m., after Sickles's brigade). Engaged strength reported ~1,000-1,315; 1,100 adopted. Sickles's Excelsior Brigade is documented present but NOT modeled - no engaged strength exists and its weight sits partly inside us_hooker (D92: never invent a number). (Verified late reinforcement; Inferred strength/timing)

reinforcements.name: Magruder's Division
reinforcements.entry: late to the field after bad guides and confusing orders
reinforcements.note: Magruder's late, piecemeal attack on the Confederate right - Cobb (partial), Barksdale, Kershaw, Semmes, G.T. Anderson, with Toombs never closing - became the main infantry effort after Armistead's movement. E52: the brigade list and ~3-hour bad-guides delay are the sourced facts; no aggregate exists, so 6,300 is an Inferred allocation toward the Verified ~30,000 CS engaged. (Verified brigades/delay; Inferred strength/timing)

reinforcements.name: Huger's Division (right flank)
reinforcements.entry: toward the narrow western approach and Malvern Cliffs
reinforcements.note: Huger's brigades beyond Armistead - Wright, Mahone (repulsed repeatedly in the Crew-house ravine), Ransom (over 400 casualties near the Crew house) - pressed Porter's left under terrain and coordination failures. E52: identities sourced, per-brigade strengths a dead end; 4,500 is an Inferred allocation toward the Verified ~30,000 (Armistead modeled separately - no double-count). (Verified brigades; Inferred strength/timing)

reinforcements.name: Whiting's Division (Jackson's support)
reinforcements.commander: Brig. Gen. W. H. C. Whiting
reinforcements.entry: from Jackson's left, too late to make the attack coherent
reinforcements.note: E52 honesty fix: Whiting's division was positioned on Lee's left in a support/artillery-cover posture and did NOT make a committed infantry assault (it still took ~175 casualties under the Union guns). Deliberately NOT raised toward the 30,000 aggregate - scaling a non-assaulting division up would misrepresent it. (Verified support posture; Inferred strength/timing)

reinforcements.name: D.H. Hill's Supporting Batteries
reinforcements.entry: brought forward into Hunt's counter-battery fire
reinforcements.note: Additional Confederate guns tried to support Hill's center assault but could not overcome the Federal ridge batteries. Universal gun model: 6 guns. (Verified context; Inferred grouping)

US.US: History holds tactically. As at Malvern Hill on July 1, 1862, the Union gun line and Porter's infantry broke the Confederate attacks before they could take the crest. The harder question comes after victory: McClellan still retreated to Harrison's Landing, ending the Peninsula Campaign.
US.CS: History holds tactically, but against your army. The Union gun line held Malvern Hill and the Confederate attacks stopped short of the crest; Lee saved Richmond only because McClellan retreated after winning the field.

CS.US: History is overturned. The Confederate assault took the plateau that Hunt and Porter historically held. In reality, Lee's attacks were beaten back by artillery, infantry, and open ground; this time the gun line broke.
CS.CS: History is overturned. Your army took Malvern Hill, coordinating the attack that historically collapsed into piecemeal assaults and artillery slaughter. Richmond's defense becomes a battlefield victory instead of a saved campaign by retreat.

endNote.draw: A bloodier stalemate than history's clear Union tactical victory - the gun line held long enough to blunt Lee's final blow, but the plateau remained contested when darkness fell.

teaching.title: Malvern Hill - Artillery, Open Ground, and Command Failure

cards.head: Henry Hunt's gun line
cards.body: Malvern Hill was not a fortress. It was a modest plateau with open fields of fire. Henry Hunt made it deadly by massing batteries along the ridge, shifting guns as ammunition ran low, and using counter-battery fire to crush Lee's planned crossfire. This is the professional artillery lesson that will matter again at Gettysburg.
cards.provenance: Verified (high) - NPS Richmond; American Battlefield Trust; Encyclopedia Virginia.

cards.head: The failed Confederate grand batteries
cards.body: Lee and Longstreet hoped two Confederate artillery positions would converge on the Federal guns and suppress them before the infantry attacked. Poor communication, brigade-attached batteries, and Union counter-battery fire ruined the plan. Of the guns available, only a handful fired effectively together at any one time.
cards.provenance: Verified (high) - Encyclopedia Virginia; NPS History Civil War Series; American Battlefield Trust map.

cards.head: Piecemeal assault by rumor
cards.body: Armistead's move against skirmishers, reports that mistook wagons for a Union withdrawal, Magruder's late arrival, and D.H. Hill's assault in the center turned Lee's plan into a sequence of isolated attacks. The soldiers were brave; the command system failed them.
cards.provenance: Verified (high) - American Battlefield Trust; Encyclopedia Virginia; NPS.

cards.head: Retreat from a tactical victory
cards.body: Malvern Hill was a clear Union tactical victory, yet McClellan ordered the army back to Harrison's Landing the next morning. The Seven Days had saved Richmond and broken McClellan's offensive nerve. The battle is therefore two lessons at once: tactical success on the field and strategic failure in the campaign.
cards.provenance: Verified (high) - Encyclopedia Virginia; NPS Richmond; American Battlefield Trust.

cards.head: The James River guns
cards.body: Union gunboats on the James threw heavy shells toward the Confederate positions. Their fire was frightening and dramatic, though imprecise; the decisive killing power came from Hunt's field batteries and the infantry on the plateau. The river mattered strategically because it gave McClellan a secure base and line of retreat.
cards.provenance: Verified (medium-high) - NPS History Civil War Series; American Battlefield Trust.

codex.title: Malvern Hill, July 1, 1862
codex.summary: The final battle of the Seven Days. Porter and Henry Hunt used Malvern Hill's open fields of fire to mass Union artillery and repel Lee's poorly coordinated attacks. The result was a Union tactical victory with heavy Confederate losses, but McClellan still retreated to Harrison's Landing, ending the Peninsula Campaign.
